content: I am a 3rd-year PhD student in the Department of Biostatistics at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health. I currently work with Dr. Ingo Ruczinski and Dr. H. Benjamin Larman to characterize antibody responses to HIV and phage components of the human microbiome.
summary: I am a 3rd-year PhD student in the Department of Biostatistics at the Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health. I currently work with Dr. Ingo Ruczinski and Dr. H. Benjamin Larman to characterize antibody responses to HIV and phage components of the human microbiome.
title: Athena Chen

summary: An introduction to JAGS and rJAGS.
title: Introduction to JAGS and rJAGS

summary: An example of data analysis summarizing concepts taught in class.
title: Data Analysis Example

summary: We used a system based on phage display to quantify antibody binding to 3,300 HIV peptides in samples from adults with early to late-stage HIV infection. The number of unique eiptopes targeted (antibody breadth) increased in the first year of infection, but then declined in those who later started antiretroviral treatment (ART); a faster rate of decline in antibody breadth was associated with a shorter time to ART initiation. By evaluating the level of antibody reactivity to each of the 3,300 peptides over time, we identified 266 peptides that had increasing antibody reactivity over time and 43 peptides had decreasing reactivity over time). These data were used to design a 4-peptide “serosignature” that predicted the duration of HIV infection. We also demonstrated that epitope engineering can be used to optimize peptide binding properties for applications such as cross-sectional HIV incidence estimation.
title: Comprehensive Profiling of HIV Antibody Evolution

content:
Battle royale games have surged in popularity in recent years. The premise of such games is as follows: players are dropped onto a fictional island and fight to be the last person standing. As they roam around the island, they loot for weapons and items crucial for their survival. Players can choose to join a game as a solo player or with a group of friends (4 players maximum). When playing solo, players are immediately eliminated when they are killed. Players not only have to worry about getting killed by other players, but they also have to stay within the shrinking “safe zone,” which effectively forces players into contact with each other. Outside of the “safe zone,” players take damage to their health at increasing rates.
The main goal of this project is to predict a solo player’s finish placement based on their in-game actions. Specifically, we wanted to answer:
 How well can we predict who will win the game? What player actions or features are most predictive of their finish placement?
summary: Identified game components associated with final performance.
title: Predicting Finish Placements in PUBG

content: HIV incidence can be determined by following cohorts of HIV-uninfected individuals and quantifying the number of new HIV cases or by using cross-sectional methods. Current cross-sectional methods of estimating HIV incidence utilize multi-assay algorithms (MAAs) to classify individuals as recently or non-recently infected. However, serologic assays that measure general antibody responses to HIV infection may be impacted by viral suppresion (innate or via antiretroviral therapy treatment (ART)), loss of CD4 T-cells, and other factors, resulting in inaccurate incidence estimates. Consequently, we are interested in developing more accurate methods for cross-sectional incidence estimation.
summary: HIV incidence can be determined by following cohorts of HIV-uninfected individuals and quantifying the number of new HIV cases or by using cross-sectional methods. Current cross-sectional methods of estimating HIV incidence utilize multi-assay algorithms (MAAs) to classify individuals as recently or non-recently infected.
title: Developing Cross-Sectional Methods to Estimate HIV Incidence

content:
Due to the complexity of cells, it is useful to use computational tools to understand and predict mechanisms of biological processes. Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs) have proven to be successful at modeling various large systems. However, to understand certain biological processes such as bacterial cell division, high spatial resolution is necessary to discern the underlying mechanisms and interactions. ODEs and PDEs, along with the Gillespie algorithm for stochastic modeling, do not provide spatial resolution at a single-particle level; they are all concentration-based methods. MCell, the Free Propagator Reweighting Algorithm (FPR), and Smoldyn are algorithms that provide single-particle resolution, but may be computationally expensive and may not necessarily yield accurate protein dynamics.
In her recent work, Athena Chen and her mentors Dr. Margaret Johnson and Dr. Osman Yogurtcu in the Johns Hopkins Biophysics department, analyzed the strengths and limitations of ODEs, PDEs, Gillespie, MCell, Smoldyn, and FPR through establishing and performing a set of benchmark tests. Though all modeling methods extracted the correct equilibrium concentrations for most of the tested reactions, the resulting protein dynamics were not necessarily correct. Simulations at high rates and large densities showed that despite providing single-particle resolution, Smoldyn and MCell do not pick up single-particle effects where the distance between two molecules affects the probability of binding. Furthermore, the dynamics given by Smoldyn and MCell are dependent on the time step selected. On the other hand, FPR correctly identified single-particle effects and yielded dynamics independent of the selected timestep.
summary: Due to the complexity of cells, it is useful to use computational tools to understand and predict mechanisms of biological processes. Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs) have proven to be successful at modeling various large systems.
title: Spatial Cell Modeling: Applications and Evaluation of Methodologies

content:
Due to the complexity of cells, it is useful to use computational tools to understand and predict mechanisms of biological processes. Ordinary differential equations (ODEs) and partial differential equations (PDEs) have proven to be successful at modeling various large systems. However, to understand certain biological processes such as bacterial cell division, high spatial resolution is necessary to discern the underlying mechanisms and interactions. ODEs and PDEs, along with the Gillespie algorithm for stochastic modeling, do not provide spatial resolution at a single-particle level; they are all concentration-based methods. MCell, the Free Propagator Reweighting Algorithm (FPR), and Smoldyn are algorithms that provide single-particle resolution, but may be computationally expensive and may not necessarily yield accurate protein dynamics.
I worked with Dr. Margaret Johnson to analyze the strengths and limitations of current state-of-the-art approaches for modeling spatial and temporal dynamics in the cell. I helped establish and perform benchmark tests for comparing the accuracy of a range of methodologies, including single-particle models like those developed in our lab, as well as differential equation based methods, and stochastic simulation methods. We evaluated the effects of molecular geometry, diffusion, and stochasticity on protein dynamics in models of gene expression and cell division.
summary: Evaluated the performance of various approaches for modeling spatial and temporal cell dynamics.
title: Spatial Cell Modeling
